tasks.prompt: Warm-up: Create a GitLab project and push the Dockerfile-equipped app from Lecture 2 to it. Confirm the code appears in the GitLab web UI on the 'main' branch.
tasks.starter:
# Run these commands from inside your app directory (the one with Dockerfile)
git init
git remote add origin https://gitlab.com/<your-namespace>/<your-project>.git
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit with Dockerfile"
git push -u origin main
tasks.solution:
# Complete sequence:
git init
git remote add origin https://gitlab.com/yourname/myapp.git
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit with Dockerfile"
git push -u origin main

# Verification: open https://gitlab.com/yourname/myapp in a browser.
# You should see Dockerfile, index.js, package.json, .dockerignore listed on the main branch.

# Also create a dev branch for experimentation:
git checkout -b dev
git push -u origin dev
tasks.explanation: The GitLab repository is the starting point of every automated pipeline run. The 'main' branch will later be the deployable branch; 'dev' (or feature branches) is where day-to-day work happens. Having both branches set up now means you can configure the pipeline to trigger only on 'dev' pushes during learning without risking the deployable branch.
hints: If 'git push' is rejected with 'remote: The project you were looking for could not be found', double-check the remote URL matches your GitLab namespace and project name exactly.
If GitLab asks for a username/password and you have 2FA enabled, you must use a Personal Access Token as the password — a regular password will not work over HTTPS with 2FA.

tasks.prompt:
Core practice: Connect GitLab to Jenkins so that pushing code automatically triggers a pipeline build.

Complete all five steps:
1. Create a GitLab Personal Access Token with 'api' scope.
2. Install the GitLab plugin in Jenkins.
3. Create a GitLab connection in Jenkins (Manage Jenkins → System → GitLab section) and test the connection.
4. In your Jenkins Pipeline job's Build Triggers, enable 'Build when a change is pushed to GitLab' and select Push Events.
5. Create a webhook in your GitLab project (Settings → Webhooks) using the URL and secret token displayed in the Jenkins Build Triggers section.

Verify by using GitLab's 'Test → Push events' button and confirming a build appears in Jenkins.
tasks.starter:
# This task is configuration-based. Use the following checklist to track progress:
# [ ] GitLab Personal Access Token created (scope: api)
# [ ] GitLab plugin installed in Jenkins
# [ ] GitLab connection created in Jenkins and 'Test Connection' returns success
# [ ] Jenkins Pipeline job has 'Build when pushed to GitLab' enabled
# [ ] Webhook URL and secret token copied from Jenkins into GitLab
# [ ] GitLab 'Test > Push events' triggered
# [ ] Jenkins shows a new build in the job history
tasks.solution:
# Step 1 — GitLab Personal Access Token
# Avatar (top-left) → Preferences → Access Tokens → Add new token
# Name: jenkins-integration  Expiry: 90 days  Scope: api
# Copy the token immediately — it is shown only once.

# Step 2 — Jenkins GitLab plugin
# Manage Jenkins → Plugins → Available → search 'GitLab' → Install
# (GitLab API Plugin installs automatically as a dependency)

# Step 3 — GitLab connection in Jenkins
# Manage Jenkins → System → scroll to 'GitLab' section
# Connection name: gitlab-saas
# GitLab host URL: https://gitlab.com
# Credentials: Add → Kind: GitLab API token → paste token from Step 1
# Click 'Test Connection' → should show 'Credentials verified'

# Step 4 — Jenkins job Build Trigger
# Open your Pipeline job → Configure → Build Triggers
# Check: 'Build when a change is pushed to GitLab'
# Events: Push Events ✓
# Note the webhook URL shown (e.g. https://<jenkins-host>/project/<job-name>)
# Click 'Generate' next to Secret Token — copy the generated token

# Step 5 — GitLab webhook
# GitLab project → Settings → Webhooks → Add new webhook
# URL: paste the Jenkins webhook URL from Step 4
# Secret token: paste the generated secret from Step 4
# Trigger: Push events ✓  → Add webhook
# Click 'Test' → 'Push events' → expect HTTP 200 response
# Switch to Jenkins — a new build appears in the job's build history
tasks.explanation: The connection rests on two matching facts: the URL that tells GitLab exactly where to send the HTTP POST, and the shared secret token that lets Jenkins verify the request is genuine. When Jenkins receives a push event, it checks the token in the request header; if it matches, the build starts. This is why the token must be copied accurately from Jenkins to GitLab — a mismatch returns a 403 and no build fires. The GitLab plugin additionally allows Jenkins to post build status back to GitLab merge requests, closing the feedback loop.
hints: If the GitLab 'Test → Push events' returns a non-200 HTTP code or times out, verify that your Jenkins host is reachable from the internet (GitLab SaaS must be able to POST to it). If Jenkins is running locally, you need a tunneling tool like ngrok to expose it: 'ngrok http 8080' gives you a public URL to use as the webhook URL.
If Jenkins shows 'Test Connection' failure, confirm the GitLab host URL has no trailing slash and the credential kind is 'GitLab API token' (not 'Username with password').

If no build appears after the test event, open the webhook in GitLab (Settings → Webhooks → Edit) and scroll to 'Recent deliveries' to see the response Jenkins sent — a 403 means the secret token does not match.

tasks.prompt: Applied challenge: Prove the end-to-end webhook trigger works with a real code push. Make a small change on the 'dev' branch, push it, and confirm Jenkins starts the build automatically — showing 'Started by GitLab push' in the console output.
tasks.starter:
# On the dev branch:
git checkout dev
# Make a trivial change:
echo "webhook trigger test" >> README.md
git add README.md
git commit -m "Test webhook trigger"
git push
# Then open Jenkins and watch the job's build history.
tasks.solution:
git checkout dev
echo "webhook trigger test" >> README.md
git add README.md
git commit -m "Test webhook trigger"
git push

# Expected Jenkins behavior within 1-2 seconds of the push:
# 1. A new build (#N) appears in the job's build history.
# 2. Click the build → Console Output.
#    The first lines should include:
#    'Started by GitLab push by <your-username>'
# 3. The build may fail if the Jenkinsfile does not yet exist in the repo —
#    that is expected at this stage. What matters is that the trigger worked.
tasks.explanation: Seeing 'Started by GitLab push' in the console output is the definitive proof that the webhook connection is working end-to-end: GitLab detected the push event, delivered the HTTP POST to Jenkins, Jenkins verified the secret token, and started a build. A build failure at this point (because there is no Jenkinsfile yet) is perfectly fine — the trigger mechanism is what this lecture is about. The pipeline content comes in the next lecture.
hints: If no build appears after the push, go back to GitLab → Settings → Webhooks → Recent deliveries to see whether the POST was attempted and what Jenkins responded.
If you pushed to 'main' instead of 'dev', check which branch the Build Trigger is configured to react to — it must match the branch you pushed to.
